is a city in [Washington County, Ohio](/wiki/Washington_County,_Ohio "Washington County, Ohio"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). It is located in [southeastern Ohio](/wiki/Appalachian_Ohio "Appalachian Ohio") at the confluence of the [Muskingum](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") and [Ohio Rivers](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), 11 miles (18 km) northeast of [Parkersburg, West Virginia](/wiki/Parkersburg,_West_Virginia "Parkersburg, West Virginia"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Marietta has a population of 13,385 people. It is the principal city of the Marietta [micropolitan area](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area"), which includes all of Washington County, and is the second-largest city in the [Parkersburg–Marietta–Vienna combined statistical area](/wiki/Parkersburg%E2%80%93Vienna_metropolitan_area "Parkersburg–Vienna metropolitan area").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Muskingum_River_Marietta.jpg/250px-Muskingum_River_Marietta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muskingum_River_Marietta.jpg)

[Muskingum River](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") near its mouth in downtown Marietta

**Marietta** is a city in [Washington County, Ohio](/wiki/Washington_County,_Ohio "Washington County, Ohio"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). It is located in [southeastern Ohio](/wiki/Appalachian_Ohio "Appalachian Ohio") at the confluence of the [Muskingum](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") and [Ohio Rivers](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), 11 miles (18 km) northeast of [Parkersburg, West Virginia](/wiki/Parkersburg,_West_Virginia "Parkersburg, West Virginia"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Marietta has a population of 13,385 people. It is the principal city of the Marietta [micropolitan area](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area"), which includes all of Washington County, and is the second-largest city in the [Parkersburg–Marietta–Vienna combined statistical area](/wiki/Parkersburg%E2%80%93Vienna_metropolitan_area "Parkersburg–Vienna metropolitan area").

Founded in 1788 by pioneers to the [Ohio Country](/wiki/Ohio_Country "Ohio Country"), Marietta was the first permanent U.S. settlement in the newly established [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), created in 1787, and what would later become the state of Ohio. It is named after [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette"), then Queen of France, in honor of [French aid in the American Revolution](/wiki/France_in_the_American_Revolutionary_War "France in the American Revolutionary War").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The area was inhabited by various native tribes of the [Hopewell tradition](/wiki/Hopewell_tradition "Hopewell tradition"), who built the [Marietta Earthworks](/wiki/Marietta_Earthworks "Marietta Earthworks"), a complex more than 1,500 years old, whose Great Mound and other major monuments were preserved by the earliest settlers in parks such as [Mound Cemetery](/wiki/Mound_Cemetery_(Marietta,_Ohio) "Mound Cemetery (Marietta, Ohio)"). Since 1835 the city has been home to [Marietta College](/wiki/Marietta_College "Marietta College"), a private, nonsectarian liberal arts school with approximately 1,200 students. Leading up to the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), the city was a station on the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistoric")]

Succeeding Indigenous cultures lived along the Ohio River and its tributaries for thousands of years. Among them were more than one culture who built earthwork mounds, monuments which generally expressed their cosmology, often with links to astronomical events.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Between 100 BC and AD 500, the [Hopewell culture](/wiki/Hopewell_culture "Hopewell culture") built the multi-earthwork complex on the terrace east of the Muskingum River near its mouth with the Ohio. It is now known as the Marietta Earthworks. Developed over many years, it had a large enclosed square, within which were four platform mounds, used for ceremonial purposes and elite residential; another square, and a larger conical mound used for burials. A walled, graded path led to the river's edge.[[9]](#cite_note-OHC-9) By the time of the historic tribes, such as the [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee"), the purposes and makers of the monuments were no longer known.

### Settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Settlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/CampusMartius.jpg/250px-CampusMartius.jpg)](/wiki/File:CampusMartius.jpg)

Campus Martius fort at Marietta, with conical Great Mound visible in background to right of tree

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/PicketedPointMariettaOH.jpg/250px-PicketedPointMariettaOH.jpg)](/wiki/File:PicketedPointMariettaOH.jpg)

Picketed Point stockade at Marietta

The government of [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") sent [Pierre Joseph Céloron de Blainville](/wiki/Pierre_Joseph_C%C3%A9loron_de_Blainville "Pierre Joseph Céloron de Blainville") in a military expedition to this area in the 18th century, and in 1749 the expedition buried six leaden plates to mark their claim to the Ohio Country. They later ceded their territory east of the Mississippi to the British after the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"). In 1798, one plate was discovered in Marietta and another was discovered near the [Kanawha River](/wiki/Kanawha_River "Kanawha River"). The Marietta Plate was replicated for what is known as the French monument which was erected in the 20th century. Before the importance of the plate was recognized, it had been partially destroyed to make [musket balls](/wiki/Musket#Ammunition "Musket").[[10]](#cite_note-monument-10)

In 1770, the future [U.S. president](/wiki/U.S._president "U.S. president") [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), then a [surveyor](/wiki/Surveyor_(surveying) "Surveyor (surveying)"), began exploring large tracts of land west of his native [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"). During the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), Washington told his friend General [Rufus Putnam](/wiki/Rufus_Putnam "Rufus Putnam") of the beauty he had seen in his travels through the [Ohio Valley](/wiki/Ohio_Valley "Ohio Valley") and of his ideas for settling the territory. By 1787, Washington owned 30,000 acres of land on the east side of the Ohio River.[[11]](#cite_note-linklater2002-11): 83

After the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the U.S. sold or granted large tracts of land to stimulate development in this area after the [Seven Ranges survey](/wiki/Seven_Ranges_survey "Seven Ranges survey") was completed in 1780s. Marietta is located just east of the southern terminus of the [Seven Ranges](/wiki/Seven_Ranges "Seven Ranges"). Marietta was founded by settlers from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") who were investors in the [Ohio Company of Associates](/wiki/Ohio_Company_of_Associates "Ohio Company of Associates").[[12]](#cite_note-Hubbard,_Robert_Ernest_pp._80,_103-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) It was the first of numerous [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") settlements in what was then the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory").[[14]](#cite_note-New_England_page_175-14) These New Englanders, or "[Yankees](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee")" as they were called, were descended from the [Puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") English colonists who had settled [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") in the 1600s and were primarily [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalist_church "Congregationalist church"). The first church constructed in Marietta was a Congregationalist church, founded around 1786.[[14]](#cite_note-New_England_page_175-14) Before the mid-1790s services were held at the fort or in Munsell's Hall at nearby Point Harmar. In 1798 the Muskingum Academy was built on the site of the 19th century Marietta Congregationalist Church. The academy building served both educational and religious purposes.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

After the war, the newly formed United States had little cash but plenty of land. Eager to develop additional lands, the new government decided to pay veterans of the Revolution with [warrants](/wiki/Warrant_(finance) "Warrant (finance)") for land in the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), which was organized under federal authority in 1787 by the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance"). Competing states had agreed to end their claims to the lands; Pennsylvania and Virginia received some lands in a settlement. [Arthur St. Clair](/wiki/Arthur_St._Clair "Arthur St. Clair") was appointed by the president as governor of the new territory. He was inaugurated on a site now marked by the [Start Westward Memorial](/wiki/Start_Westward_Memorial "Start Westward Memorial").

The [Ohio Company of Associates](/wiki/Ohio_Company_of_Associates "Ohio Company of Associates") had supported provisions in the ordinance to allow veterans to use their warrants to purchase the land. They bought 1.5 million [acres](/wiki/Acres "Acres") (6,100 km2) of land from [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_United_States "Congress of the United States").[[12]](#cite_note-Hubbard,_Robert_Ernest_pp._80,_103-12) On April 7, 1788, [48 men](/wiki/American_Pioneers_to_the_Northwest_Territory "American Pioneers to the Northwest Territory") of the Ohio Company of Associates, led by General Putnam, arrived at the confluence of the [Muskingum](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") and [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") rivers.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The site was on the east side of the Muskingum River, across from [Fort Harmar](/wiki/Fort_Harmar "Fort Harmar"), a military outpost built three years prior.

Bringing with them the first government sanctioned by the US for this area,[[17]](#cite_note-Hildreth-17) they established the first permanent United States settlement in the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory").[[18]](#cite_note-Hulbert_Vol_I-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Hulbert_Vol_II-19) (Older European settlements in the Northwest Territory region include [Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan "Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan"), 1668; [Cahokia, Illinois](/wiki/Cahokia,_Illinois "Cahokia, Illinois"), 1696, [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), 1701; [Kaskaskia, Illinois](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"), 1703, [Ouiatenon, Indiana](/wiki/Ouiatenon,_Indiana "Ouiatenon, Indiana"), 1717, [Prairie du Rocher, Illinois](/wiki/Prairie_du_Rocher,_Illinois "Prairie du Rocher, Illinois"), 1720; [Vincennes, Indiana](/wiki/Vincennes,_Indiana "Vincennes, Indiana"), 1732, [Clarksville, Indiana](/wiki/Clarksville,_Indiana "Clarksville, Indiana"), 1783, [Martin's Ferry, Ohio](/wiki/Martin%27s_Ferry,_Ohio "Martin's Ferry, Ohio"), 1785, Fort Finney/[Jeffersonville, Indiana](/wiki/Jeffersonville,_Indiana "Jeffersonville, Indiana"), 1786, most settled by ethnic French colonists from Canada.) The Americans named Marietta in honor of [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette"), the Queen of [France](/wiki/France "France"), who had aided the colonies in their battle for independence from Great Britain.

The settlers immediately started construction of two forts: [Campus Martius](/wiki/Campus_Martius_(Ohio) "Campus Martius (Ohio)"), whose former site is now occupied by the [museum of the same name](/wiki/Campus_Martius_Museum "Campus Martius Museum"), and [Picketed Point Stockade](/wiki/Picketed_Point_Stockade "Picketed Point Stockade"), at the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the Muskingum and Ohio rivers. At the same time, the settlers started developing their community, platted according to plans they had made in Boston.

In 1788, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") said:

> No colony in America was ever settled under such favorable auspices as that which has just commenced at the Muskingum. ... If I was a young man, just preparing to begin the world, or if advanced in life and had a family to make provision for, I know of no country where I should rather fix my habitation....[[20]](#cite_note-Sparks_IX_385-20)

The families of the settlers began arriving within a few months. By the end of 1788, 137 people populated the area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/PUTNAM_exb.jpg/250px-PUTNAM_exb.jpg)](/wiki/File:PUTNAM_exb.jpg)

[Rufus Putnam](/wiki/Rufus_Putnam "Rufus Putnam") was George Washington's chief engineer. After the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), he led the first settlers to Marietta, erected the [Campus Martius](/wiki/Campus_Martius_(Ohio) "Campus Martius (Ohio)") fort, and established the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") as free soil - no slavery.

In 1789, the United States signed the [Treaty of Fort Harmar](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fort_Harmar "Treaty of Fort Harmar") with several Indigenous tribes that occupied areas of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), to settle issues related to trade, as well as the boundary between their lands and United States settlement. The US did not address the Indigenous people's major grievance about American settlers moving into their lands, particularly in the [Western Reserve](/wiki/Western_Reserve "Western Reserve"), where there were disputes over land. Although Congress authorized Governor [Arthur St. Clair](/wiki/Arthur_St._Clair "Arthur St. Clair") to give land back to the Indigenous people, he did not do so. Conflict increased as the Indigenous people tried to push the settlers out. After years of warfare in the region, they were defeated. The US signed the [Treaty of Greenville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Greenville "Treaty of Greenville") (1795) with the Indigenous people, which secured the safety of settlers to leave the forts and develop their farms. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The settlers held services regularly and chartered the first church in 1799.[[21]](#cite_note-21) It was a [Congregational](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") institution; its charter was unusually inclusive due to the varied religious backgrounds of its members. The congregation constructed the first church building in 1807.[[14]](#cite_note-New_England_page_175-14) The original church burned in 1905 and another constructed in its place in 1906. The church, First Congregational Church United Church of Christ, is the longest continuously worshiping congregation west of the Alleghenies.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Education was important to the settlers, many of whom had been officers during the Revolution. During that first winter, they began a basic school for the children at Campus Martius. In 1797, settlers founded Muskingum Academy. The town had numerous [abolitionists](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States"), and Ephraim Cutler was instrumental as a state delegate in 1802 at the state convention in swaying the vote for the state to be free of slavery.[[23]](#cite_note-Yeager-23)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Marietta_Works_Squier_and_Davis_Plate_XXVI.jpg/250px-Marietta_Works_Squier_and_Davis_Plate_XXVI.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marietta_Works_Squier_and_Davis_Plate_XXVI.jpg)

1837 Survey of Marietta Earthworks

Townspeople organized and chartered [Marietta College](/wiki/Marietta_College "Marietta College") in 1835. It was used as a station on the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad") to help [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves") escape from the South.[[23]](#cite_note-Yeager-23)

The settlers preserved the Great Mound, or *Conus,* by planning their own [cemetery](/wiki/Mound_Cemetery_(Marietta,_Ohio) "Mound Cemetery (Marietta, Ohio)") around it. They also preserved the two largest platform mounds, which they called *Capitolinus* and *Quadrophenus.* The former was developed as the site for the city library.[[9]](#cite_note-OHC-9) As of 1900, the [Mound Cemetery](/wiki/Mound_Cemetery_(Marietta,_Ohio) "Mound Cemetery (Marietta, Ohio)") had the highest number of burials of Revolutionary War officers in the nation, indicating the nature of the generation that settled Marietta.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Marietta's location on two major navigable rivers made it ideal for [industry](/wiki/Industry_(manufacturing) "Industry (manufacturing)") and [commerce](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce"). [Boat building](/wiki/Boat_building "Boat building") was one of the early industries. Artisans built oceangoing vessels and sailed them downriver to the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and south to New Orleans and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). In less than two decades after settlement, the [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat") had been developed, and was also constructed here. Brick factories and [sawmills](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill") supplied materials for homes and public buildings. An iron mill, along with several foundries, provided rails for the growing [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") industry; the Marietta Chair Factory made [furniture](/wiki/Furniture "Furniture").

Interest in the prehistoric culture that built the Marietta Earthworks continued. The complex was surveyed and drawn by [Ephraim George Squier](/wiki/E._G._Squier "E. G. Squier") and [Edwin Hamilton Davis](/wiki/Edwin_Hamilton_Davis "Edwin Hamilton Davis"), whose large project on numerous prehistoric mounds throughout the Ohio and Mississippi valleys was published by the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") in 1848 as *[Ancient Monuments of the Mississippi Valley](/wiki/Ancient_Monuments_of_the_Mississippi_Valley "Ancient Monuments of the Mississippi Valley").* It was the first book published by the Smithsonian. Their drawing above shows the plan of the original complex, which "included a large square enclosure surrounding four flat-topped pyramidal mounds, another smaller square, and a circular enclosure with a large burial mound at its center."[[9]](#cite_note-OHC-9) The walled, graded path, called by the settlers the *[Sacra Via](/wiki/Sacra_Via "Sacra Via")*, led from the largest enclosure to the lower river's edge. This pathway was destroyed in 1843 during mid-nineteenth century development.[[9]](#cite_note-OHC-9)

### Railroads and oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Railroads and oil")]

Local development began with the [Belpre and Cincinnati Railroad](/wiki/Belpre_and_Cincinnati_Railroad "Belpre and Cincinnati Railroad") (B&C); it was founded in 1845. It was intended to connect from [Belpre, Ohio](/wiki/Belpre,_Ohio "Belpre, Ohio"), the next town downriver, to a planned [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") (B&O) spur to [Parkersburg](/wiki/Parkersburg,_West_Virginia "Parkersburg, West Virginia"). But, for years, the Virginia government did not allow the B&O to construct track south of [Wheeling](/wiki/Wheeling,_West_Virginia "Wheeling, West Virginia"). In 1851 developers changed the Ohio state terminus to Marietta and changed the name of the railroad to the [Marietta and Cincinnati Railroad](/wiki/Marietta_and_Cincinnati_Railroad "Marietta and Cincinnati Railroad") (M&C) that year. The right-of-way for an alternate connection to the B&O extended upriver from Marietta to [Bellaire, Ohio](/wiki/Bellaire,_Ohio "Bellaire, Ohio"). The M&C was bankrupt by 1857, but construction of track continued west to reach [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"). The first through-train from Cincinnati ran on April 9, 1857. The M&C got out of bankruptcy in 1860.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1871, the Ohio Valley Railroad was formed and for the next two years built tracks going north for 103 miles. Their home office was in Marietta, with treasurer offices in Pittsburgh. The Ohio Valley railroad was reorganized as the Marietta and Cleveland. The Pennsylvania Railroad in its expansion later purchased the railroad and its right-of-way between Marietta and Bellaire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Passengers traveling between Marietta and Parkersburg, Virginia (now West Virginia) had to take a steamboat for the 14 miles between the two towns and transfer. With help from the B&O and the Baltimore City Council, the Union Railroad finally connected Marietta to [Belpre, Ohio](/wiki/Belpre,_Ohio "Belpre, Ohio") in 1860. Later absorbed by the B&O, this section of track is still in operation (2008), with unit coal trains providing most of the traffic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The planned [bridge](/wiki/Parkersburg_Bridge_(CSX) "Parkersburg Bridge (CSX)") from Parkersburg across the Ohio River to Belpre was finally built 1868–1870 by the B&O, as part of its main line from Baltimore to [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri").[[25]](#cite_note-25) This cut Marietta off from traffic and trade, although it retained local and Ohio service. In the early 20th century, 24 passenger trains served Marietta each day, most of which ran on the PRR tracks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[William P. Cutler](/wiki/William_P._Cutler "William P. Cutler") was a major figure in the M&C. He also backed the [Union Railroad](/w/index.php?title=Union_Railroad_(Ohio)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Union Railroad (Ohio) (page does not exist)") and the Marietta, Columbus and Cleveland Railroad, among other local railroads. Cutler served as General Manager and as President of the M&C for many years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1860, oil was first drilled in the Marietta region. Oil booms in 1875 and 1910 made investors rich, who constructed numerous lavish houses in town, of which many still stand.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The Dawes brothers of Marietta founded the [Pure Oil Company](/wiki/Pure_Oil_Company "Pure Oil Company").[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] All four brothers became nationally prominent businessmen or politicians: [Charles Gates Dawes](/wiki/Charles_Gates_Dawes "Charles Gates Dawes"), [Rufus C. Dawes](/wiki/Rufus_C._Dawes "Rufus C. Dawes"), [Beman Gates Dawes](/wiki/Beman_Gates_Dawes "Beman Gates Dawes") and [Henry May Dawes](/wiki/Henry_May_Dawes "Henry May Dawes"). Charles Dawes was elected in 1924 with President [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge") to serve as the 30th [Vice President of the United States](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") (1925–1929). In 1925, he shared the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), based on his work on the [Dawes Plan](/wiki/Dawes_Plan "Dawes Plan") and relieving an international crisis in 1923 related to German reparations after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

In 1880, the first [Putnam Street Bridge](/wiki/Putnam_Street_Bridge "Putnam Street Bridge") was opened to connect Marietta to Fort Harmar. It provided the first free crossing of the [Muskingum River](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Map of Marietta, 1923

As transportation advanced along railroads and highways, Marietta was initially passed by. From 1868 to 1870, the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad built a bridge to connect [Parkersburg, West Virginia](/wiki/Parkersburg,_West_Virginia "Parkersburg, West Virginia"), and Belpre; and the [National Road](/wiki/National_Road "National Road") went further north through [Zanesville](/wiki/Zanesville,_Ohio "Zanesville, Ohio"). The [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") expanded in the late 19th century and had a station in Marietta, running 26 daily trains between Marietta and [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1917, a group of 23 young men organized the Marietta College Ambulance Unit, which went on to serve in France, landing at [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux") in June; four died in battle. A plaque was given by the government of France to the city of Marietta and dedicated to the Marietta College Ambulance Unit in 1939, during the Northwest Territory Sesquicentennial Celebration.[[10]](#cite_note-monument-10) In 1939, the [Sons and Daughters of Pioneer Rivermen](/wiki/Sons_and_Daughters_of_Pioneer_Rivermen "Sons and Daughters of Pioneer Rivermen") was established in Marietta during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") to celebrate the city's substantial river history and its people. Two years later the [Ohio River Museum](/wiki/Ohio_River_Museum "Ohio River Museum") was opened.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), passenger service decreased as the railroads restructured and the federal government invested in highway construction. The last rail passenger service ended in 1953. Marietta was relatively isolated from new travel routes until 1967, when [I-77](/wiki/I-77 "I-77") was opened with close access to the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 2016 Ohio State of the State address by governor [John Kasich](/wiki/John_Kasich "John Kasich") was held at People's Bank Theater on April 6.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 8.75 square miles (22.66 km2), of which 8.43 square miles (21.83 km2) is land and 0.32 square miles (0.83 km2) is water.[[28]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-28)

The [Muskingum River](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") and [Duck Creek](/wiki/Duck_Creek_(Ohio) "Duck Creek (Ohio)") flow into the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") at Marietta. The area is part of the [Appalachian Plateau](/wiki/Appalachian_Plateau "Appalachian Plateau") which covers the eastern half of Ohio. The Appalachian Plateau consists of steep hills and valleys and is the most rugged area in the state. The area is within the [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") of the [Western Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Western_Allegheny_Plateau_(ecoregion) "Western Allegheny Plateau (ecoregion)").[[29]](#cite_note-hort.purdue.edu-29) This portion of the state has some of Ohio's most abundant mineral deposits.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Marietta was affected by the [Great Flood of 1913](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1913 "Great Flood of 1913").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate in this area is characterized by humid summers, cold winters, and evenly distributed precipitation throughout the year that can not be accurately predicted because of the amount of water in the Ohio Valley. According to the [Köppen Climate Classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Classification "Köppen Climate Classification") system, Marietta has a [Humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"), abbreviated "Dfa" on climate maps.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Marietta had a population of 13,385. The median age was 39.4 years, 18.2% of residents were under the age of 18, and 20.1% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 91.1 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 89.0 males age 18 and over.[[40]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-41)

99.5% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.5% lived in rural areas.[[42]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-42)

There were 5,758 households in Marietta, of which 23.7% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 34.0% were married-couple households, 22.4% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 34.6% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 39.5% of all households were made up of individuals and 16.9% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[40]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-40)

There were 6,472 housing units, of which 11.0% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.8% and the rental vacancy rate was 8.2%.[[40]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-40)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"),[[43]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-43) there were 14,085 people, 5,828 households, and 3,215 families residing in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,670.8 inhabitants per square mile (645.1/km2). There were 6,519 housing units at an average density of 773.3 per square mile (298.6/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 94.9% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 1.3% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.3% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 1.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.5% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 1.5% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 1.1% of the population.

There were 5,828 households, of which 25.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 37.9% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 13.0% had a female householder with no husband present, 4.3% had a male householder with no wife present, and 44.8% were non-families. 37.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 15.7% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.14 and the average family size was 2.80.

The median age in the city was 39 years. 18.9% of residents were under the age of 18; 16% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 21.1% were from 25 to 44; 25.7% were from 45 to 64; and 18.4% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 46.9% male and 53.1% female.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=). *(March 2017)* |

Sewah Studios, a producer of historical markers, was founded in Marietta in 1927.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The company produced the United States' first aluminum historical markers,[[45]](#cite_note-45) and currently produces about 1,200 markers per year for historical societies across the country.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Marietta is home of the longest-running [ferromanganese](/wiki/Ferromanganese "Ferromanganese") refinery in North America, [Eramet](/wiki/Eramet "Eramet") Marietta Industries Inc., the only ferromanganese refinery in the United States until recently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*], and leader in [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese") emissions.[[36]](#cite_note-residents-36)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/2007_Sternwheeler_Festival.jpg/250px-2007_Sternwheeler_Festival.jpg)](/wiki/File:2007_Sternwheeler_Festival.jpg)

Ohio River Sternwheel Festival

The annual Ohio River Sternwheel Festival was founded in 1976,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and features [Sternwheeler](/wiki/Paddle_steamer "Paddle steamer") ships gathering on the Ohio River in Marietta. The event attracts an estimated 100,000 attendees per year.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Other boating events include the Ralph Lindamood Memorial Regatta, the Marietta Invitational Regatta, and the "Head of the Muskingum".

A Marietta Civil War Reenactment is held annually, and features Union and Confederate reenactors battling across the Muskingum River. Goodfest is a music festival held at Goodfellows Park.

In 2023, Marietta installed a gallery of public murals by local artists, which reflected Marietta's history.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Library")]

Marietta has a public library, a branch of the Washington County Public Library.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Government")]

Marietta uses a [mayor-council government](/wiki/Mayor-council_government "Mayor-council government"). The mayor is a full-time position; the seven city council members and the city council president are all part-time positions. The mayor is Joshua Schlicher.[[3]](#cite_note-Riggenbach-3)

Marietta is represented by Republican [Kevin Ritter](/wiki/Kevin_Ritter "Kevin Ritter") (District 94)[[51]](#cite_note-51) in the Ohio House of Representatives, and by Republican [Brian Chavez](/wiki/Brian_Chavez "Brian Chavez") (District 30) in the Ohio Senate.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Marietta falls within [Ohio's 6th congressional district](/wiki/Ohio%27s_6th_congressional_district "Ohio's 6th congressional district"), which is currently represented by Republican [Michael Rulli](/wiki/Michael_Rulli "Michael Rulli").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marietta,_Ohio&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Education")]

As of 2021, the Marietta City School District operates three elementary schools and one building that houses a middle/high school, [Marietta High School](/wiki/Marietta_High_School_(Ohio) "Marietta High School (Ohio)").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

[Marietta College](/wiki/Marietta_College "Marietta College") and [Washington State College of Ohio](/wiki/Washington_State_College_of_Ohio "Washington State College of Ohio") are both located in Marietta.

[Interstate 77](/wiki/Interstate_77 "Interstate 77") runs east of Marietta connecting it to [Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio"), to the north and [Charleston, West Virginia](/wiki/Charleston,_West_Virginia "Charleston, West Virginia"), to the south. Five state routes run through Marietta. These are: [Ohio State Route 7](/wiki/Ohio_State_Route_7 "Ohio State Route 7"), [Ohio State Route 60](/wiki/Ohio_State_Route_60 "Ohio State Route 60"), [Ohio State Route 26](/wiki/Ohio_State_Route_26 "Ohio State Route 26"), [Ohio State Route 550](/wiki/Ohio_State_Route_550 "Ohio State Route 550"), and [Ohio State Route 676](/wiki/Ohio_State_Route_676 "Ohio State Route 676").

Marietta is served by [Mid-Ohio Valley Regional Airport](/wiki/Mid-Ohio_Valley_Regional_Airport "Mid-Ohio Valley Regional Airport") in [Williamstown, West Virginia](/wiki/Williamstown,_West_Virginia "Williamstown, West Virginia"), which has three flights a day Monday through Friday from [Charlotte Douglas International Airport](/wiki/Charlotte_Douglas_International_Airport "Charlotte Douglas International Airport").

Marietta's River Trail [bike path](/wiki/Bike_path "Bike path") is a two lane, paved trail that spans over four miles. While built for [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling"), it is heavily used by pedestrians as well. The trail runs along the [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") and [Muskingum](/wiki/Muskingum_River "Muskingum River") rivers and connects various points of interest throughout Marietta, including downtown and multiple parks.
